title: Rabbit vaccination schedule for UK rabbits
subtitle: What to vaccinate against, when, and with which product.
excerpt: UK rabbits face two serious viral diseases: myxomatosis and Rabbit Haemorrhagic Disease (RHD), which has two strains. Both are endemic in wild rabbits and transmissible to domestic stock through insect bites, direct contact, and contaminated surfaces. Vaccination is the only reliable protection.
sourceNotes: RWAF vaccination guidelines, rabbitwelfare.co.uk. RCVS Practice Standards rabbit health guidance. MSD Animal Health Nobivac Myxo-RHD product information.

glossaryTerms.definition: A fatal viral disease of rabbits, transmitted by biting insects and direct contact with infected animals. Symptoms include swelling of the eyelids, lips, and genitals. No treatment; vaccination is the only protection.
Rabbit Haemorrhagic Disease, caused by a calicivirus. There are two strains: RHD1 and RHD2 (a newer variant first detected in the UK in 2013). Both cause sudden death in unvaccinated rabbits.

content.text: are both present in wild rabbit populations across most of the UK. An unvaccinated domestic rabbit near a wild population is at continuous risk. Rabbits must be vaccinated by a vet. Owners cannot give these vaccines themselves because the products are prescription-only.
As of 2024, most domestic rabbits in the UK are vaccinated with Nobivac Myxo-RHD Plus. A single annual injection covers myxomatosis, RHD1, and RHD2.
Before Nobivac Plus, two separate vaccines were needed, given at least two weeks apart. Ask your vet which products are current for your area.

Kittens can be vaccinated from five weeks of age. A single injection of Nobivac Myxo-RHD Plus provides protection from around three weeks after vaccination, which lasts twelve months. Annual boosters are required to maintain protection.

Timing the annual booster matters for outdoor rabbits. Myxomatosis transmission peaks in late summer through to October, when rabbit flea and mosquito populations are highest. Booking the booster in June or July ensures maximum protection through the high-risk period.

In a large meat rabbit colony, annual vaccination of every animal is a significant cost. Many smallholders vaccinate breeding does and bucks only. Grow-out kits go to slaughter before the next booster is due, so the risk window is short.
This works well for indoor or fly-proofed systems. Outdoor meat rabbits in areas with known outbreaks carry more risk. Talk to your vet about a plan that fits your setup.
Keep a simple record for each animal: date vaccinated, vaccine name and batch number, vet's name, date next booster due. The batch number matters for product recall tracing.
